bytes.topic: Engine Choice — Unity vs Unreal vs Godot vs Bevy, trade-offs per project type
bytes.title: Godot Engine Choice: Weighing Unity, Unreal, Godot, and Bevy for 2D Projects
bytes.body: Godot excels in 2D game development due to its lightweight architecture and built-in Node system. When choosing an engine for a 2D project, consider Godot's SceneTree, which allows for efficient node management. For example, using the `get_node()` function to access nodes in the tree can simplify your code. In contrast, Unity and Unreal Engine are more geared towards 3D development, while Bevy is a Rust-based engine that may require additional setup for 2D projects. Godot's open-source nature and large community also contribute to its appeal for 2D game development.

bytes.topic: Scene Graph — hierarchical object structure, parent-child relationships, transforms
bytes.title: Godot Scene Graph — Mastering Hierarchical Object Structure
bytes.body: Godot's scene graph is a hierarchical structure of nodes, where each node has a parent-child relationship with others. The Node2D or Node3D class serves as the base for all nodes, providing properties like position, rotation, and scale. When a node is added as a child to another, its transform is relative to its parent's transform. This allows for efficient management of complex scenes. Godot's built-in APIs, such as get_parent() and add_child(), simplify node manipulation. Understanding the scene graph is crucial for creating complex game worlds and levels.
bytes.code_snippet:
var child_node = Node2D.new()
add_child(child_node)
child_node.position = Vector2(10, 20)
print(get_parent().name)

bytes.topic: Physics — rigidbody, colliders, triggers, physics layers, performance implications
bytes.title: Godot Physics Optimization: Rigidbody, Colliders, and Layers
bytes.body: Godot's physics engine can be optimized by carefully configuring Rigidbody, Colliders, and Physics Layers. Rigidbody nodes provide collision detection and response, while Colliders define the shape of the object. Physics Layers allow for fine-grained control over collision interactions. To improve performance, use the `set_collision_layer` and `set_collision_mask` methods to filter out unnecessary collisions. Additionally, use triggers to detect collisions without affecting the physics simulation.

bytes.topic: Godot — nodes and scenes, GDScript vs C#, signals system, composition pattern
bytes.title: Godot — Mastering Node Composition with Signals
bytes.body: Godot's node system allows for complex scene hierarchies. When combining multiple nodes, the composition pattern is crucial for maintainable code. GDScript and C# can be used interchangeably, but GDScript is generally more idiomatic for Godot development. The signals system enables decoupling of nodes, making it easier to manage complex interactions. By connecting signals, nodes can react to events without knowing the implementation details of the emitting node.

bytes.topic: Unreal Engine — Blueprints vs C++, Actors, Components, UObject garbage collection
bytes.title: Godot: Optimizing Performance with GDScript
bytes.body: Godot's GDScript is a powerful scripting language, but it can lead to performance issues if not used efficiently. To avoid garbage collection, use Stack-based data structures and minimize dynamic memory allocation. In Godot, using `preload` and `load` functions can help reduce the overhead of dynamic loading. For example, when creating multiple instances of a scene, use `preload` to load the scene once and then instantiate it multiple times. This approach reduces the number of dynamic allocations and improves performance.

bytes.topic: pygame — game loop in Python, sprite groups, event handling, rapid prototyping
bytes.title: Godot — Implementing Pygame-like Game Loop in GDScript
bytes.body: Godot's scene system and signal handling can be used to replicate Pygame's game loop and event handling. By using the _process() function, developers can create a continuous loop similar to Pygame's game loop. Additionally, Godot's Node class has a set of built-in signals for handling events like mouse and keyboard input. To group sprites, Godot provides the Node2D class, which can be used as a container for multiple sprite nodes. This allows for easy management and manipulation of sprite groups.

bytes.topic: Input Handling — input abstraction, action maps, controller support, input buffering
bytes.title: Godot Input Handling — Simplifying Controller Support with Action Maps
bytes.body: Godot's input system provides a robust way to handle user input through action maps. Action maps allow you to abstract input actions from specific input devices, making it easier to support multiple controllers. You can create action maps using the Project Settings > Input Map tab. To handle input buffering, use the `Input.is_action_just_pressed()` and `Input.is_action_just_released()` functions. For more complex input handling, utilize the `InputEvent` class to capture and process raw input events.

bytes.topic: Bevy — ECS architecture, systems, components, resources, Rust-powered
bytes.title: Godot — Understanding Entity-Component-System Architecture with Bevy Inspiration
bytes.body: Godot's scene system can be seen as a simplified Entity-Component-System (ECS) architecture. While not as robust as Bevy's ECS, Godot's Node system allows for a similar approach. Nodes act as entities, and scripts attached to them can be considered components. However, unlike Bevy, Godot doesn't have a built-in system for managing resources. To implement an ECS-like system in Godot, you can create a custom Node that acts as the entity, and attach scripts to it that represent components. This approach allows for a more modular and scalable game architecture.

bytes.topic: UI in Games — world-space vs screen-space UI, Unity Canvas, UMG in Unreal
bytes.title: Godot UI Optimization: World-Space vs Screen-Space UI
bytes.body: Godot provides two primary ways to render UI elements: world-space and screen-space. World-space UI is rendered as part of the 3D scene, allowing for immersive and interactive experiences. Screen-space UI, on the other hand, is rendered on top of the 3D scene, ideal for HUD elements, menus, and other overlaying interfaces. To achieve this in Godot, use the `Control` node for screen-space UI and the `MeshInstance` node with a `CanvasLayer` for world-space UI. This approach enables efficient rendering and reduces performance overhead. Godot's `Camera` node also plays a crucial role in determining the rendering order and perspective of world-space UI elements.

bytes.topic: Multiplayer Foundation — client-server vs P2P, state synchronization, rollback netcode
bytes.title: Godot Multiplayer Foundation: Client-Server vs P2P with State Synchronization
bytes.body: Godot's built-in networking features support both client-server and peer-to-peer (P2P) architectures. When choosing between these, consider the game's requirements: client-server is more scalable, while P2P reduces latency. For state synchronization, Godot's RPC (Remote Procedure Call) system can be used to send and receive data between nodes. To implement rollback netcode, use Godot's built-in `Network` class to handle packet sending and receiving, and the `PhysicsServer` to simulate physics on the server-side.

bytes.topic: Prototyping vs Production — when to switch engines, scope management, jam vs shipped game
bytes.title: Godot: Prototyping vs Production — When to Switch Scenes
bytes.body: Godot's scene system is ideal for prototyping, allowing for rapid testing and iteration. However, as projects grow, scene management becomes crucial. When prototyping, use the built-in SceneTree to manage nodes and scenes. For production, consider using a custom Scene Manager script to handle scene loading and unloading, reducing memory usage and improving performance. Use the `get_tree().change_scene()` function to switch between scenes. To optimize scene loading, use the `preload()` function to load scenes ahead of time.

bytes.topic: Performance — draw calls, batching, occlusion culling, profiling in engine
bytes.title: Optimizing Godot Performance with Batched Draw Calls
bytes.body: Godot's rendering pipeline can be optimized by reducing the number of draw calls. This can be achieved by using batched rendering, where multiple meshes are rendered in a single draw call. The `VisualServer` API provides the `mesh_instance_set_batching_mode` function to enable batching for a specific mesh instance. Additionally, occlusion culling can be used to reduce the number of objects being rendered. Godot's built-in `OcclusionCuller` class can be used to implement occlusion culling. Profiling tools such as the `Profiler` class can help identify performance bottlenecks in the game.

bytes.topic: Build & Distribution — platform-specific builds, store submission, CI for games
bytes.title: Godot Exporting: Creating Platform-Specific Builds
bytes.body: Godot's export system allows developers to create platform-specific builds for their games. The Export dialog in Godot provides options to customize the export process, including setting the export path, template, and debug mode. To create a platform-specific build, select the desired platform in the Export dialog and configure the export settings as needed. Godot supports exporting to various platforms, including Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS. The `ProjectSettings` class in GDScript provides access to project settings, including export settings. By using the `ProjectSettings` class, developers can automate the export process and create custom export scripts.

bytes.topic: Game Loop — update, fixed update, late update, frame rate independence with delta time
bytes.title: Mastering Godot's Game Loop: Understanding Update, Fixed Update, Late Update, and Delta Time
bytes.body: In Godot, the game loop is divided into three main types: update, fixed update, and late update. The update function is called every frame, but its execution time is not guaranteed. Fixed update is called at a fixed interval (usually 1/60th of a second), ensuring consistent execution time. Late update is called after all physics simulations have been processed. Delta time is used to account for variable frame rates by scaling time-sensitive operations. When implementing the game loop, use delta time to ensure smooth gameplay and physics simulations.

bytes.topic: Camera Systems — follow camera, camera shake, culling, FOV considerations
bytes.title: Godot Camera Systems — Follow Camera, Shake, Culling, and FOV Tweaks
bytes.body: To implement a smooth follow camera, use `Camera2D` and `KinematicBody2D` with `follow_distance` set to a suitable value. For camera shake, apply a `CameraShakeInput` to the camera node. Culling can be achieved by using `Camera2D`'s `use_depth_test` property. When adjusting FOV, use `Camera2D`'s `field_of_view` property, which accepts degrees. You can also use `Camera2D`'s `orthographic_size` property to adjust the vertical FOV for 2D scenes.

bytes.topic: Unity — MonoBehaviour lifecycle, prefabs, ScriptableObjects, asset database
bytes.title: Godot: Understanding Node Lifecycles and ScriptableObjects
bytes.body: Godot's scene tree is composed of nodes, which can be created, modified, and deleted dynamically. When a node is instantiated, it goes through several lifecycle events, including `_ready()`, `_process()` and `_physics_process()`. These events can be used to initialize and update node properties. ScriptableObjects, on the other hand, are designed for storing reusable data. They can be used to create prefabs, which are instantiable scenes that can be reused throughout the project. By attaching a ScriptableObject to a node, you can manage its properties and behavior more efficiently.

bytes.topic: Asset Pipeline — importing models, textures, audio, LOD, asset optimization
bytes.title: Optimize Your Godot Game with Efficient Asset Pipeline
bytes.body: To reduce loading times and improve performance, Godot's asset pipeline is optimized for importing models, textures, and audio. When importing 3D models, use the `MeshInstance` scene node and `load` function to load .OBJ or .GODOT files. For textures, use the `Texture` resource and `load` function to load .PNG or .JPG files. Audio files are loaded using the `AudioStream` resource and `load` function. Level of detail (LOD) is implemented using the `LODNode` scene node and its `set_lod` method. To optimize assets, use Godot's built-in texture compression and mesh simplification tools.

bytes.topic: Audio — positional audio, audio mixing, music vs SFX, compression formats
bytes.title: Godot Audio 101: Positional Audio, Mixing, and Compression
bytes.body: Godot's `AudioStreamPlayer3D` node allows for positional audio, creating an immersive experience. To mix audio, use the `AudioEffect` node with `Reverb` or `Distortion` effects. Separate music from SFX using the `AudioStream` node or by creating separate `AudioStreamPlayer` instances. For compression, use the `FLAC` or `OggVorbis` formats, which are supported by Godot natively.
